item.title: Secure Non Smart Phone
item.description:
In modern times, people communicate with each other via smartphones, mostly. Whether it's WhatsApp, iMessage, Matrix, email, Signal, or even SMS and phone calls, most people use a smartphone. That means I have to use a smartphone to receive communications from these smartphone-owners, as well. But I don't want to. Opting out of the smartphone would also mean opting into communication surveillance (and lower quality communication overall), and I do not want that.

There are non-smart-phones ("dumb" phones, as they're called now) that are intelligently designed and fairly non-creepy, like the Light Phone and Punkt MP02, but those for some reason don't put security - or, if your family/friends only use some smartphone app, communication at all - on their list. Also, these devices with their basic SMS/phone call only communication are bizarrely expensive.

Smartphones are bad. That's something we can all agree on here. But the need for smartphones these days cannot be understated. Personally, I feel trapped in the walled garden full of parking apps, 2-factor authenticator apps, communication apps, apps necessary for work - and I'd guess most other people do too.

I propose a tool that is a communication device that puts security and privacy first, but is also not a smartphone. The communicator should look and act like a smartphone, but have only a small set of pared-down first-party programs that facilitate communication. The closest comparison would be an iPhone with only four core functions:

  1. Messaging. This encompasses voice calls, video calls, and text-based messages. It will integrate with Signal, Tox, Matrix, or whatever is deemed the best most friendly but also secure way to communicate. It should have robust APIs and support integrations from external services, so you can get non-human alerts. This is helpful for people in my line of work.
  2. Notes. People frequently have need to jot down a quick note. This program will store plain-text files in a folder and will be visible over USB. Encryption would be considered here.
  3. Settings. Of course you need a way to set up accounts, connect to networks, change accessibility preferences, set up a mobile hotspot, view data usage, and manage the darn thing. Updates for core software will be handled from here as well.
  4. Maps. One of the more common smartphone features people rely upon and would miss is a maps program. The maps program would allow offline navigation, encouraging local storage of map areas, and possibly integrate with ride-hailing services.
Functions not included in this device would be things like a music player, camera, web browser, video player, app store, and other features best left to other, larger devices.

Speaking of physical characteristics, here are the main goals for the communicator's physical shape:

  • Small size. Most phones these days are ridiculously huge. This should be iPhone 4S size, about. Since touch screens are less expensive than buttons (and allow for greater modularity) this would not have a physical tactile keyboard.
  • Removable, replaceable parts. The communicator's battery should be easily accessible. It should have an easily swappable SIM card (or two), and optionally a microSD card slot for storage of larger maps, or mass storage accessible via MTP.
  • Headphone jack with TRRS support. It's nice to walk around hands-free with a headset while taking a call, instead of performing neck-gymnastics or constantly switching hands. The communicator should be able to take advantage of modern headphones with in-line mics.
  • Standard connectivity. It should use modern WiFi and cellular radios, replaceable as new tech comes out. As many parts as possible should be pogo-pin connected to the mainboard, to facilitate easy removal. The charger should be USB-C or micro-USB, and that should be a choice users can make to suit their needs. The battery should be something that already exists and can be easily sourced, like an existing smartphone battery, or a BL-5C battery.
All in all, I'm not a manufacturer or a materials scientist. A device like this would be fantastic, and would be my ideal handheld communicator. Minimal required functionality, a true off switch, inexpensive upgrades or part replacements, and obviously 100% open source hardware and software.

This is an opinionated device, with very little functionality, made specifically for a small subset of people who have a complicated love/hate relationship with technology like mine. I don't expect it ever to happen, but it's nice to put into words. A man can dream, right?

Yes, I'm aware of the PinePhone. It gets pretty close, but it's enormous and more functional than I'd like. The device I'm proposing is much stricter and more opinionated than that.

item.title: Good Beers
item.description:
Here's some good beer's I'd recommend, and the time of year in which they should be consumed. I don't like drinking dark beer in the spring or summer time, since to me, it feels like something you should have with a stew by the fire in winter. Just like hot coffee isn't great on a summer afternoon (cold brew for that!) and a milkshake might not be a great time on a cold day, different flavors of beer should only be quaffed when it's their perfect time of year. Of course, this is all my opinion, and you may have different taste from me. This list will be updated as I try new things.

  • Guinness - not a great beer, but the Best Beer. Drinkable in any season, but preferable fall/winter. Nice and smooth, a classic dark beer. Not very complex.
  • Sierra Nevada Hazy Little Thing - a fantastic go-to spring or summer beer, as is any hazy IPA. Sweet and fruity, simple, and just all around lovely.
  • Backwoods Pecan Pie Porter - Possibly my favorite "weird flavors" beer. Very nice flavor for before/after a cold night walk. Great in October..
  • Blue Moon - surprisingly I just had one of these a few weeks ago. I don't often go for non "craft" beers, but I had one after a run in the sun, and it was fantastic.
  • Deschutes Hand Up - Feels like a mix between an IPA and something a little more full-bodied and flavorful, like and ale. I've only had a few but something I'm warming to. 6/10, only because it's got a higher ABV than it has any right to. Good all year.
  • Left Hand Milk Stout - Nitro or GTFO! This is like a Guinness++. Nothing worse and everything's better. I restrict stouts to winter but with this one it's hard.
  • Arrogant Bastard - You probably wouldn't like it anyways, but it's worth a shot. It's a strong, tall boy. Fall.
  • Rogue Voodoo Doughnut - Had this one years ago, and I still remember it fondly. It was weird (of course it was) and I like weird, but it was a little too much for me. Probably don't drink it during a Kansas summer. That's likely what ruined it.
  • Riverbend Life's a Peach And Then You Die - it's a Peach Milkshake IPA. It's thick, cloudy, sweet, but not overly cider-y, still very beer-y. Delicous. Spring & summer. Possibly the one on this list I'm most fond of, mostly because my wife likes them too. Typically she likes ciders but this one's a great middle ground.

item.title: Home Surveillance Tier List
item.description:
So you want home security cameras? We'll start off with the easy/flaming garbage/entry level, then go all the way to Ultimate Ascendent Gamer God-Tier.

Easy/Flaming Garbage:

  • Nest/Ring/Arlo - basically self-surveillance. THEY should be paying YOU for this. Don't buy. The police can access this stuff whenever they want, and even if you like the police, this is bad. Also you pay a monthly fee.
  • Hikvision - a company sponsored and staffed by the Chinese government. Even though they're technically and physically good, it's like allowing the Chinese government into your home, email, banking, and private thoughs. There's a reason they cost 1/3 the price of something good.

"I did slightly more research than clicking on an ad" tier:

  • Wyze cameras - they're ok. Still pretty creepy spyware, but at least it's not going directly to big tech. The low cost and free monitoring makes it at least worth considering.

1337 Hackerman tier:

  • Raspberry Pi + Open Source solutions - Shinobi, ZoneMinder, Blue Iris, etc. are all pretty fun little hacks to run on a low cost credit card sized computer. These solutions cost next to nothing, and are highly modular. Add whatever cameras you like! You can even combine them with high tier stuff like Axis cameras, or low tier stuff like Wyze cameras (with the RTSP firmware).

"I have more money than sense" Tier:

  • Ubiquiti UniFi cameras - good stuff. They don't operate with other systems, you have to buy all their stuff, they send loads of telementry you can't turn off, but this is very high quality gear that will last for decades. Real businesses deploy this stuff. Ubiquiti is good. If you like Apple products and don't like learning, buy these.

Ultimate Ascendent Super-Saiyan God Tier:

  • Build your own NVR setup with A NAS/home server and RTSP/ONVIF cameras - you care about this stuff. It matters to you. You have 8 disks in RAID, offsite backups, a UPS, a PoE Switch, and 17 Axis cameras bolted to your house. You run OpenBSD on your desktop.

This list is a joke.

item.title: Home Security Cameras
item.description:
There's two ways to do video monitoring of your home and its surroundings: the easy way, and the right way. I'll explain a) why you should care, b) why the easy way isn't actually that easy, and c) how to achieve both setups. Really, you should do this reasearch for yourself, since these things change all the time, and everyone has different requirements. A setup like this is going to require that you do some work and use your brain. It's going to be a learning experience, and possibly frustrating. At the end you'll get something really good out of it, and the knowledge you gain along the way will help you be a more valuable resource to yourself and others in the future. This podcast episode is a bit of required listening if you're interested in these products/solutions.

First, you need to ask yourself a few questions.

  • Do you need constant, real-time video of your home (inside/out) accessible wherever you are, especially outside the home?
  • Do you care who has access to that data? Would it make you sad to know that a bunch of people you don't know/trust could access your footage?
  • Do you want historical data? Would you like to look back at old footage? How far back do you want to go? 30 days? Is 7 days good enough?
  • Are you willing to learn, and put some time and effort into setting this up correctly, or do you just want someone else to handle it?
If you are more or less apathetic to those points, and it doesn't really matter, then get a Nest/Ring/Arlo setup. It's cheap, you'll be surveilled by the government, police, contractors for Google/Amazon, but whatever, you don't care, right? Stop reading right here. (These are bad systems. They grant other people control over your surveillance, and they make you dependent on their whims. Don't make these products things that you depend on.)

First off, if you want cameras to record videos, cameras themeselves can't store and transmit video. The cameras need to connect back to a computer that can store, analyze, and transmit the video. If you want this to be an even remotely good experience, you need to buy something with an ethernet port and a set of big hard drives. To start out, go for a Synology. I have one. They're great products, easy to set up, and easy to keep secure. You can connect any type of ONVIF camera to them, the low-end ones even have powerful CPUs to transcode/transmit all that video, and you can also use spare old smartphones as surveillance cameras. Awesome! This NS1218 is a decent start if you just want the cheapest good option. If you want something that's a better long-term option, buy the bigger version, the DS918+. It is much more expensive, but will last longer, and will afford you more fault tolerance.

Now you have your computer. But it needs storage. For drives, buy some Seagate IronWolf NAS drives. 2TB is good enough if you don't need massive capacity, but 4TB is a great sweet spot (and that's when you get drives with bigger caches, anyway). Price accordingly.

Next, you need cameras! What type of cameras should you get? Cameras that support the ONVIF standard are the best things to buy, since they're interopreable with any system you could use. You want to buy PoE cameras (power over ethernet) and wire them to a PoE switch. This is the best way to ensure high availability and a consistent connection. Anything connected over Wi-Fi is going to be slower, have higher latency, be more effected by external forces (storms, cars parked in the driveway, people standing in from of the camera) and it will also degrade the performance of your home network. (and vice versa - if you're streaming 4K video your cameras are going to crap out)

Obviously you could use any RTSP/ONVIF camera, but what is out there? The best stuff is Axis gear, but it's very expensive. Ubiquiti's UniFi cameras are also great, but aren't as customizable. If you want something cheap, go with Wyze cams. You can manually flash RTSP firmware and get them going, which is a good cheap option. They connect over Wi-Fi which is also less than idea.

With all your pieces together, you'll have to install Synology's surveillance station on your NAS, and get your cameras plugged in. If you want fault tolerance, set up RAID, and get a UPS. You can get a cheap little APC desktop UPS, plug in your PoE Switch and NVR, and you'll be good to go if there's a power blip. The switch will power back on when it gets power, and the Synology can be configured to start up after it loses power.

Now here's the hard part - networking setup. Ideally, you'd have 3 vlans, minimum - the cameras will be on a camera-only vlan, with no internet access, client isolation, and no access to anything else. The only thing they'll be able to do is access one port on your server vlan, where your NVR lives. Then you can have a third vlan which is your "regular computer" vlan, where your desktop/laptops live. That can connect into the server vlan, but the server vlan can't get to the computers (unless you need it to for some reason). Then, you have a VPN server in your server vlan (whether that's built in on the synology or on a separate server, your choice), and that is your only external hole. This is a reasonably secure setup that isolates things that might be untrustworthy from the rest of your secure zones.

Anyway, these are just my thoughts I wrote down to think about this stuff myself. Haven't bought or used any of these so I can't say myself what's the best. This is just my afternoon's research.

item.title: How To Disable All Windows 10 Built-In Ads
item.description:
This article caught my attention recently.

These always make me laugh. This is valid until a) they add more ad locations, b) they don't turn the settings back on automatically, c) they don't cripple the experience in other ways, require more interaction. It's always a race against how horribly Microsoft can cripple Windows, and if you can keep up with it via changing settings every week, fighting against dark patterns, or registry hacks.

Here's my 1 step list to get rid of ads in Windows:

1. Install GNU/Linux.

At this point nobody that's not an enterprise really needs Windows. GNU/Linux is very good and will probably work better for all but a small minority of computer users.

The common excuses people give are

  1. I need X specific program
  2. I play video games
  3. I don't want to learn something new/put in any effort/use my brain at all

You can use all but the most niche software on GNU/Linux, or there's an appropriate alternative for it. (Yes, that does mean use GIMP instead of Photoshop. If it has problems, you can fix them, or report them to the developers. That's how Open Source Software works.)

Most video games are playable via Steam+Proton, that is, you install Steam, check one box in Steam settings, and install your games. If they aren't, then play them on a console, or don't play them. You don't need to play video games. There also exist a bunch of open source games you can play. Might as well try out Xonotic, 0AD, Minetest, or even Roguelikes (nethack, crawl, rogue).

And finally if you are so opposed to freedom and just really want to keep the shackles on, I can't help you. Everything in life requires learning, and if you won't take the time to learn something incredibly valuable, go jump in a lake.

item.title: Port Forwarding and Dependencies
item.description:
Time for a tale of mystery and horror. Every so often my parents call me about computer/network/services problems, and I always solve them free of charge since because they dealt with me for 18+ years, and also I love them and stuff.

My dad has this thing in his house that's an internet-connected box that controls physical things in his house (so, already a bad idea; we've all seen that episode of Mr. Robot). It's got a bunch of outputs - CEC, IR, (and RS232 and Ethernet, neither used at this point) that control the entertainment center stuff and switch between inputs. He and my mom can control it on the iPad (which is what he likes). There's also a big physical remote with BlackBerry-style wheel and back button on it, which is AWESOME. Anyhow, the computer box portion of it broke.

So this internet connected mystery computer that has unlimited access to his home network (even though I gave him a guest network with isolation!) needs to get an update from the manufacturer to re-enable iPad control because that was the part that broke. So he calls the technicians and they say they can do it via teamviewer, since it's corona time, and nobody wants visitors. He calls me today, frantically asking for the admin password for his router (which I mysteriously have, and he even more mysteriously does not) so he can give it to a technician. I obviously tell him no.

But then he explains that the technician is going to remote into his mac, change his router settings to set up two port forwards into this mystery box, and then he'll update it from outside.

Anyway, my dad conferences me in with this guy, and since I can't drop everything and offer my dad a better solution in the next 20 minutes, I guess this thing has to happen. I insist the technician remove the port forwards after he is done with them, since there's little else I can do. He agrees. My dad already has his grill, doorbell, ancient home security system, Christmas lights, generator, and cooking temperature probes on his home network, so it's a lost cause at this point. I'll struggle against it when I have more time.

While we're on the call, the technician causally mentions that most of the time, they don't have to have port forwards, because they usually put VPN servers in these boxes. "One button, and then bang, I'm in your home network!" Excuse me?! This box doesn't need to be Internet connected or even network connected - it's just an IR repeater - but yet, here we are.

Anyway, moral of the story - given the least charitable explanation, how comfortable are you with the stuff in your home? You should strive for less dependence on technology, especially technology of the mysterious kind. If you don't know how it works and you can't repeat it, don't use it! If you can't service it yourself, and it's a proprietary box that has full access to something you'd be hesitant to give an adversary full access to, don't use it! If facts presented as writ make the thing sound creepy, it probably is. And if it's a lynchpin of your home networking or entertainment setup, you're dependent on it.

I strive to remove these things from my life while others these days try to add them in - and I'm supposed to be the "tech guy". Strange times we live in.

P.S. Also, if you must compromise your family's safety, make sure it's at least for something cool. This ain't it, chief.

item.title: Ubuntu Derivatives
item.description:
A heavy criticism of a the dozens/hundreds of Ubuntu-based distros is that they're just "Ubuntu + a custom PPA", or even worse, just "Ubuntu + custom wallpapers". There are certainly distributions out there like that (which no one should be running), but I belive more often than not the larger ones do provide some real value and differentiate themeselves from Ubuntu proper.

To be clear, lots of the Ubuntu-based systems really are just Ubuntu + a custom ppa, or a tweaked Ubuntu full of someone else's software. But a real stand-out derivative isn't just something you could get better in one of the official flavors - usually the group behind it writes their own software, makes their own applications, or tweak the kernel in their own way for better hardware support. Unlike the lesser derivatives, they don't just pile other peoples' stuff on Ubuntu and call it good.

Which of the high-profile Ubuntu-based distros out there don't add something significant to Ubuntu? Let's take a look at the more popular ones and see if they can differentiate. I exclude the offical flavors from this for obvious reasons.

  • Linux Mint provides Cinnamon, their own desktop environment. They provide Timeshift, a very good backup program, and some great GUIs for doing complex packaging things that you can usually only do in the command line in Ubuntu. It's based on only Ubuntu's LTS, but they also maintain a Debian-based version. Not only that, they also partner with hardware vendors and make the MintBox, a fantastic computer. I think they're on 3, now. They've been doing this for years. These are significant additions.
  • ElementaryOS provides a much simpler GUI. However, they've written their own app store, created a consistent Human Interface Guidelines document for their platform, encourage and provide documentation for native development, support actually paying developers of FOSS, and are trying to adopt a way to bring this to other distros (search for "appcenter for everyone"). The curated experience, lack of desktop configurability, and overall UI is sometimes called very "Mac-like", but that's usually as a put down more than anything. The small team at Elementary is not only trying to make their OS better. They're encouraging native Linux Desktop development and encouraging users to pay devs for good stuff. These are significant additions, though less "program in your OS" and more "positive addition to the landscape of Desktop Linux".
  • System76's Pop!_OS (that hurt to type) is one of the few Ubuntu-based distros that doesn't stick only to LTS releases. Their additions are pretty straightforward: a Kernel that works bettery with their systems, a driver manager for their systems, useful tweaks to the GNOME UX via extensions they wrote, power profiles for laptops with hybrid Intel   Nvidia GPUs, Coreboot firmware for their laptops (technically not part of the distro) and an updater for said firmware (actually part of the distro), and now with 20.04 a custom tiling window manager extension for GNOME. They also support Flatpaks and Elementary's app store program. Their repos contain newer version of handy developer tools, nice addons like Lutris for gamers, new versions of the Arduino IDE, and some other stuff I haven't used. They make what I like to think of as "Ubuntu for Human Beings". Their tweaks make Ubuntu a much nicer place to live for the average user, especially if that user runs a system76 laptop.
  • Zorin OS has this badass looking systems management tool . Haven't used it so that's all I know about it. It looks like they're more focused on the enterprise, which is cool. Not something we see a lot of desktop distros doing. They also request you pay for their "Ultimate" edition, which seems like a viable business model, I guess. Gets a pass from me.
  • KDE Neon - basically Kubuntu + Backports PPA on the LTS instead of latest, minus a bunch of packages. The "KDE Experience Distro". Basically their proving ground. Basically a tool they made for themselves. Ok! Kinda an "Ubuntu + PPA" situation, but basically meant as an internal testing too, but they were gracious enough to give it to the masses. Gets a pass.
  • Linux Lite, Peppermint OS, other "lightweight" Ubuntu-base distros: they fill a nice by being super lightweight, but that niche is also served better by things like Lubuntu/Xubuntu.

Overall, the larger Ubuntu derivatives, while taking most (if not all) of their packages from Ubuntu proper, do actually seem to add some value. And the smaller ones, while probably not adding much outside of just how they're configured, they do offer value in the base configuration to their users. That's why the flavors exist - take a desktop environment, add some well-done configuration, set a custom theme, throw in some matching programs, and presto! you have yourself a "distro". When the alternative is Arch or bone-stock desktop environments, these smaller distros, based on a distro based on a distro, make sense.

After all, Ubuntu's just buiding on top of Debian's solid foundation, and they all need Linux and GNU. They're all a derivative of something - and there's nothing wrong with that.

item.title: New Laptop
item.description:
After years of crappy craigslist machines and dying batteries, I finally decided to purchase myself a new laptop. (Yes, I did get a new-to-me T430 a few months ago, but that was free, and the battery doesn't work). I decided that since I was going to give money to a company, it should be a company that makes good things and cares about freedom. And, I couldn't buy a laptop and not have coreboot at this point.

I bought a System76 Lemur Pro. It is a lightweight 14-inch black laptop that is about the same size as my 12-inch X230. It has 8GB of RAM (with an extra slot available), a 10th-gen i5 U-series CPU, an Intel AC Wi-Fi card, and 2 NVMe drive bays. The embedded controller (the chip that handles keyboard and touchpad events to name a few) and the BIOS are all open source. They use Coreboot with UEFI.

Details out of the way, here we go. First, I had to open it up and add my extra NVMe drive. There's 12 phillips screws on the bottom. No tricks, it just pops off. 12 screws is a lot but I won't be doing that often. Second, I plugged in its power adapter. There's a light next to the plug that glows orange when charging, and green when charged. It also blinks green when the laptop sleeps. It can also charge via USB-C.

Opening the lid is not possible with one hand but the hinge is pleasant and the lid doesn't bounce much. The laptop has no logos internally, and the external logo is a sticker that I peeled off immediately, given it would probably have come off in my backpack. So, this is the matte-black no-branding laptop of my dreams.

The display is the first thing anyone notices. The bezels are slim, there's a webcam/mics up top, and the display is a matte 1080p IPS panel. Perfect for the size. It's clear, sharp, and gets both really bright and dim.

The touchpad is a relatively decent touchpad. It tracks well and clicks from almost anywhere on the touchpad. It's almost as good as my 2012 Macbook. The keyboard is good, but has much less travel than I'm used to on a thinkpad or desktop mechanical keyboard. It's backlit with 5 levels of backlighting the lowest being very low, which is great. The keyboard does not have a caps lock indicator light, which is fine-ish (I either remap or don't use caps lock anyway).

Given the port situation on many modern lapops I wasn't expecting Thinkpad-levels of connectivity, but it comes really close. No ethernet, but if you want that, System76 has a Galago Pro model for you. Otherwise the two models are quite similar (the Galago just has less battery and more ports).

Pop!_OS, System76's operating system, is installed on the drive in OEM mode, and lets you install to the rest of the disk when you boot up. It thoughfully provides a recovery partition, so if you want to blow away your install and start fresh, you don't have to boot from USB. I first ran the installer in attempt to put it on the smaller, secondary disk, but it instead put it on the first disk (during install I selected the smaller one, but an 'lsblk' showed it had set up the install targets on the larger one). (Possibly human error?) I download the ISO from their site, installed it to my second 256GB disk, and on the main 500GB disk, I put Fedora (my OS of choice).

Fedora works great on this machine. No complaints, no dmesg errors, no extra drivers, no weird fan noises. I run the sway window manager primarily (and GNOME3 when I need something more full-featured) - the only change over my previous install was I had to install the "light" package, which controls backlight a la xbacklight on xorg.

Overall, this is a fantastic laptop on which to run GNU/Linux. It was a bit expensive, but I couldn't imagine putting money toward a better purpose - a small black brandless laptop running coreboot and Linux. Only thing it's missing is a trackpoint. It almost fills the Thinkpad-sized hole in my heart!

item.title: Automate These Things
item.description:
Friction in daily life is not good. Automation alleviates some friction, reducing stress, and creating more time for leisure and creativity. Many things can be automated. I have some opinions on what should ( and should not) be automated.
Here are a bunch of tasks where I've attmpted to reduce friction by automation.
  • Phone, text, email, and other alerts. I've set up filters, rules, and alerts that only really ping me when I want to be pinged.
  • News. My RSS reader (liferea) refreshes when I open it every morning, and I maintain a set of curated and low-volume feeds. I don't get caught up in infinite scroll and only see what I want.
  • Operating system updates. Follow this guide and it's pretty simple. On my servers, unattended-upgrades takes care of security updates, letting me choose when to apply the "regular" updates. My laptop running Fedora automatically downloads updates with dnf-automatic and lets me choose when to install them. Both OSs are very stable and haven't really ever broken irreparably on me, so I don't have to think about my computers much. Whenever they ask me to update, I do it, and it never takes more than 5 minutes and a normal reboot.
  • Backups. Once configured, backups should run forever and be reliable. I run Syncthing and Duplicity separately and back up to two locations. One is stored on a RAID, which is backed up to cold storage quarterly. My "cold storage" backups are not automated, but those I like to do on purpose, and they are stored in a secure location, locked up.
  • Software builds in testing/non-production environments. If you want testbox.local to have the latest version of your app every single day without you having to do it, Jenkins the heck out of it!
  • Monitoring and alerting. Cacti and Twilio make a great combo. Grafana and Nagios are also good monitoring applications.
  • OS installation. Chef, Ansible, JuJu, and Kickstart all provide GREAT ways to get a new box up and running without having to manually install software, partition your disk, or set up your network adapters.
Of course, automating things is almost as important as what NOT to automate. Some things need human intervention and oversight, for now.
  • Physical security. This includes your garage door. For obvious reasons, this shouldn't be a system that's easily fooled into unlocking itself while you're not at home. Presence detection via Bluetooth is sketchy and spoofable. If someone hacks your AD Domain Controller, you don't want a badge auto-provisioned and mailed to an address, and you don't want all your worker's badges not working if you screw up and delete an OU.
  • Cooking. Don't play with fire, kids!
  • Major software or operating system upgrades. Don't let your computers surprise you with a broken NTP service or 100% CPU utilization while you're on vacation.
  • Environmental changes. At my last job we had several air handlers that would rotate every Wednesday at 9AM. This frequently caused problems and interrupted someone's work day. It should have been a monthly (if that) process that was performed manually. Heating and cooling should operate within thresholds, but not make drastic changes without alerting you. A blown sensor could result in a 125F server room and you're none the wiser.
Of course, this non-exhaustive list is just my opinion based on experience. There are probably good arguments, and I'd love to hear them.

item.title: Toying with macOS
item.description:
A mini-review of macOS from the perspective of a Linux user


A few years ago when I switched to using Linux on the desktop full-time, it was definitely not as complete and usable a desktop computing experience as the Mac. Windows 10 was easily worse (in the early days; it's become a little better, if you use the LTSC version) and the Mac was fantastic. I started using Linux more seriously after macOS 10.10 (the one with the iOS 7-like blurry flat redesign came out) and switched to it full time a couple years later. 10.9 was peak macOS for me, and it just got harder for me to stomach after that. Maximizing got taken away; I discovered tiling window managers and loved them, and even Windows has that; more settings got jumbled for no reason; iCloud became practically manadtory or forced in half the apps, and I didn't want to use it; and obviously I got super silly paranoid.
I've always kept a few spare laptops around (when I was using macOS full time, I kept a spare Windows machine for old games), and since 2017 the Mac has been in the pile of spares. I pulled it out since I'm going to be using a Mac for work soon, and wanted to see how it works nowadays.
I would give kudos to Apple for supporting recent macOS versions on an almost 8-year-old machine, but I regularly use the latest versions of Fedora and Ubuntu on machines that are older than that, and they perform better, so that's moot.
It does perform surprisingly well on a standard 750GB HDD, and save for long boot times, operates as smoothly as it does on an SSD. It's very cool and quiet, has decent battery life, charges fast, doesn't stutter and operates well doing daily tasks. All the hardware works and it has a plethora of ports, a little LED battery meter on the case, and a DVD drive in a very compact package. It's not really all that impressive, since that's the pretty low bar I expect things to clear, but it gets the basics right.
The GUI is fine. I really do like tiling and workspaces, and the Mac is okay at those. I have to use the trackpad instead of the keyboard, but I assume there are decent keyboard shortcuts I've not looked up for things like "go to workspace n" or "move application to workspace n". I hear it has picture-in-picture, but have not tried it yet. I wonder if I could do that with a terminal instead of a video. Overall, it's less configurable than GNOME3, even, and that's saying something. Every little app costs money (things that are just normal GNOME extensions or built-in features) and I don't want to pay for something that might not be good, or might be spyware.
The worst part is the lack of package management. I rely HEAVILY on dnf/apt/flatpak/snap to do automatic updates for all my programs/system at once, and lacking a good package manager built-in is a huge flaw of the platform. I've installed Homebrew since it's what I remember using prior, and it's still good. It'll take some getting used to. Nice to have an open-source way to install and update things like keepassxc, wireshark, ffmpeg, tmux, etc.
The best part is that it's a Unix system (I know this!) and the terminal application is REALLY GOOD. They've switched to ZSH by default (seriously? Why?) but it's easy to switch back to bash or fish. I could probably install a plethora of ncurses apps on a VPS and live in an SSH connection and be perfectly happy with macOS.
Conclusion: I'm a Free Software diehard, so I'll keep using Ubuntu/Debian/Fedora forever, but macOS is good if you don't have the option to use Desktop Linux. Since I don't have to pay for the inevitable keyboard repair (it being a work computer), I guess I'll be content with macOS!

item.title: Coreboot on the T430
item.description:
Traditional computer BIOS programs are either spyware, featurless, or horrifically out of date. A few problems are evident with these systems:

  • The manufacturer never updates the BIOS and never cared about it. Critical features are missing and bugs are present.
  • The manufacturer injects programs or other spyware into the BIOS to change your operating system.
  • The manufacturer's BIOS either has glaring security issues or requrires specific versions of Windows to update, therefore rendering it unsuitable for use.

In order to solve these problems, you need a different BIOS. The manufactureres like to prevent modifications, but since most chips can just be read/flashed externally, that's not a huge problem if you're willing to do a lot of work for your freedom and security.

Coreboot is one of my favorite open soucre projects. It's a free replacement to commercial BIOS/UEFI systems. I've got it on my X220, X230 (courtesy of the Skulls project), and now the T430! In order to flash the T430, it needs to be totally taken apart. Since I just got a brand-new T430 and was going to repaste it and clean it anyway, I decided to flash Coreboot on it as well. In for a penny...

Anyway, now I have a coreboot family. Yay!

Special thanks to this blog bpost and of course all the guides out there for the T420 and X230. Splitting the flash into 2 separate chips was the trickiest part, along with figuring out which way to align the clip. Your mileage may vary with all that. My chips were different from his.

item.title: Software Projects
item.description:
Recently I have been working on a few software projects. Here is a summary of them.
  • AlephOne Marathon Launcher: remember Bungie's Marathon games? There's an open-source continuation located here but it requires the game to be run from the command line. Obviously I'm not terminal-averse, but this was fun to code. I also made a PPA for Ubuntu 18.04.
  • Link Saver: ever used Instapaper or Pocket? Basically a bookmark saver but you paste the URL in the terminal. It can save an offline file too. Pretty basic so far but I made it for me to use.
  • 2-factor Auth Anywhere: A portable set of ImageMagick scripts that basically took oathtool and put it into a little .jpg. I was working within the constraints of a) the device in question not having a real physical keyboard and b) the screen being low-res and small. It was more or less a fun exercise but nothing to take too seriously.
  • sfetch: a neofetch-like prompt that is MUCH smaller and lighter-weight. Made since I have a lot of systems I SSH into on a regular basis, and it's nice to have a little stats output right when I log in. As said in the repo, neofetch was too heavy for my poor-man's server fleet of raspberry pi zeros. This works better.

Mostly these are self-serving things that I made just to have fun!

item.title: Long-Term Goals
item.description:
We're all getting older. Me too - I got older again recently. What's the plan here?

Most these days are just trying to make it through the day. Life's a chore and so much of it has to be spent on overhead, and at the end of it all, there's not much to show for it. So having a few things in mind to shoot for, to be always on the back burner, is critical - if you're not motivated and excited about making it through the day, what's the point?

Here's a few of my back-burner perpetual goals:
  • Always keep learning! If there's something that I encounter that I don't know, I'll take the time to learn it if it's going to be important. Even if it's not related to my work, it's more important to broaden my skillset. My wife teaches me various crocheting and sewing skills, I've forced myself to learn some networking principles, and I've been picking up a musical instrumet recently.
  • Constantly examine life for optimization. Even if I've been doing thigns a certain way for decades, if there's a better way that someone shows me, switch to that! In college I switched a few writing habits, and now I'm attempting to change up some driving habits to be safer on the road and put less wear on my vehicle.
  • Work outside the comfort zone. For me this primarliy means talking to people (oooo, scary!) and maintaining social relationships. I've historically been bad at this, so forcing myself to go have friends can be difficult, but it is ultimately rewarding, and the alternative is much worse!

My long-term plans are pretty hazy right now. I don't know what I'm going to be doing in two weeks and how things are going to end up. But as long as I keep learning, keep improving, and maintain my high-quality relationships with awesome people, I've got a feeling things are gonna work out great. Like my dad always used to say - "Attitude and Effort!"

item.title: Amazon Story
item.description:
The Amazon Story I tell this little tale to my friends and family about how I got wrapped up in the whole technological privacy movement. I cared a little bit before, but mostly only from a technical interest perspective. Now I'm concerned and draw a hard line on this stuff. Here's why! This Wikipedia link has pictures of most models of Echo and a more detailed and objective set of descriptions for each.
2007 - Amazon launches the Kindle. This is widely seen as a good move. They still make these things and hardcore readers love them. I don't need to explain why Kindles (the e-paper black and white ones) are good.
2011 - In Feburary, Borders, a popular bookstore chain, files for bankrupcy. Amazon's eaten their lunch.
2011 - In September, Amazon launches the Kindle Fire, a tablet that's much cheaper (and smaller) than an iPad. Amazon markets it primarily as a way to read books (odd, given that half their Kindle marketing favorably compares the e-ink antiglare Kindle screen to the backlit LED glossy glare-prone tablet screens of the day) and it's a pretty good seller.
Shortly before the launch of this tablet, Apple decides that Amazon's Kindle app can no longer link to their ebook store, making the iPad a much worse choice for Amazon purchasers. These tablets are also still made to this day, having gone through several iterations with various features - high quality HD displays, good speakers, different sizes and aspect ratios, and enormous bubbly silicone cases for child-proofing. They are still marketed as reading devices, though they can also perform the function of an Echo, and they make it very easy to buy Amazon stuff - their primary function.
This was the beginning of Amazon's modern electronics manufaturing era. The Kindle still seems like a side business, but the "Fire" line and the Echos are more in line with Modern Amazon.

2014 - The Echo launches. It is a large black paper-towel-sized tube that is plugged into a wall outlet, connected to WiFi, and listens for commands on its highly sensitive seven-microphone array. It's basically an Orwellian telescreen, and people paid $150 for the privilege of being spied on. People said, "Okay."
Amazon said, "Okay!"


2016 - The Echo Dot launches. It's an Echo but much shorter (think hockey puck-sized), cheaper, and with a worse speaker. Amazon practically gives them away in frequent sales and bundles. They want one in every room in your house so you can always have a chance to ask Alexa (the name of the voice that talks from the tube/puck) to buy you paper towels or tell you how to make chicken soup.
People said, "Okay."
Amazon said, "Okay!"
(At the same time, the "Amazon Tap", a battery-powered bluetooth speaker version of the Echo, launches. It's not nearly as popular, but now you can take your spyware on the go. It just wasn't as useful.)

2017 - The Echo Look launches. It is an Echo (micrphones, wall power, WiFi) with a camera built in that Amazon tells you to place in your closet so it can watch you dress. It will offer outfit suggestions (and purchasing suggestions via Amazon.com) to complement your style. Most men are creeped out, because most men don't care about fasion. Women see a use for it.
People said, "That's a little creepy, but okay."
Amazon said, "Okay!"

2017 - Also in 2017, the Echo Show launches. It is an echo, but with a 7" tablet screen and a webcam. It cannot be used as a tablet since it has no battery, and requires wall power. The headliner feature was "Drop In", which enabled Echo Show owners to video call each other, but it doesn't ring, it just automatically starts the call. You can "Drop In" to your friend's kitchen at any time. This insane behavior was default-on.
People said, "That's super creepy, also why not just plug in your tablet and yell at it?" But it was still purchased and is a successful product.
Amazon said, "Okay, we'll roll back the Drop In feature and turn it off by default."
People accepted this and Amazon was not lynched that day.
2017 - Amazon buys Whole Foods in June.
2017 - The Echo Spot was released in September. It is a small sphere with the front chopped off and it looks like a tiny cute alarm clock. It is a miniature Echo Show, with a 2-inch screen that gets super dim. It is meant to be used as a bedside alarm clock, with a camera and microphones that you point at your bed.
Surprisingly no one found issue with this.
People said, "Okay."
Amazon said, "Okay!"

2017 - The nightmare never ends - in December 2017, Amazon partnered with various IOT Video Camera, Doorbell, and Door Lock manufacturers (several of which it later purchsed) to introduce Amazon Key, a service that lets Amazon delivery drivers open your door and throw your package inside your house.
A service that lets Amazon delivery drivers (NOT background-checked US Postal Service/UPS/FedEx employees, but regular folks with a station wagon and a smartphone app) enter your home.
People said, "Okay, hang on a second. This is SUPER creepy."
Amazon said, "Yeah, I know. I heard you talking about it this morning on the Echo in the bathroom as I watched you put on those new boots from the Echo Look that you bought from the Amazon app while you were laying in bed last night - also watched that on your Echo Spot. You snore a lot. I'll show you a suggestion for breathe right strips next time you're in the app."

Do you want any of this in your life? Other companies have only followed suit.
I won't tell you what to do, but know that in 2014 when these things started happening, I had already made my decision.

item.title: Windows Defender
item.description:
I'm always hearing about Windows 10 being awful - people don't like the forced updates, telemetry, ads in the start menu, etc, but in my experience it's not really that different than past windows versions once you get past the first boot.

Yeah, there's a lot to dislike about Windows 10 - the ads (especially if you pay for a key) are disrespectful, gross, and slimy, and they're everywhere - start menu, lock screen, notifications... endless ads! They really show you how much Microsoft values its desktop customers. Cortana is largely useless and I've never seen a single person actively engage with that feature. The built-in dark patterns leading you to enabling a Microsoft account for everything are pretty wild. The lack of bitlocker drive encryption on the home version is outrageous - you have to pay for the pro upgrade to use that.

Windows 10 isn't any more intrusive or workflow-breaking than 7. The telemetry is basically the same, but in 10 they actually expose some controls to the user, so many people think that it is the only Microsoft OS with any telemetry. (Side note - if you turn off telemetry in Windows 10, it will, after a time, turn it back on for you. Same with updates. You have no control.)
The update process is very similar, and I've seen Windows machines (7, 8, 10) reboot in the middle of work. The ads in the start menu is sort of new, but every Windows version tightens the screws a little bit, so it was just a matter of time. Each time you update, there are new little annoyances attached to your machine. And there's a new build every 6 months that breaks something new! These aren't isolated incedents, either - Windows 10 1809 was pulled from release twice, and even the Server 2019 builds were unavailable while Microsoft fixed this massive issue that their thousands of programmers and scores of "Windows Insider" testers couldn't catch.

I've opted out of using Windows anywhere but work, and using the enterprise version mitigates most of the bad stuff. I've seen comments from readers on Ars Technica (really!) and reddit where users defend Windows 7 as this bastion of privacy and security, like it's incapable of phoning home to Microsoft, and many people brag about disabling updates and using Windows 7 until long after EOL. Doesn't make any sense to me why, if you're not going to jump ship to macOS or Linux, why you'd continue to use a proprietary OS that you've made insecure.

Anyway. Windows rants always happen when Microsoft breaks RDP with a new update, which happens about twice a year.

item.title: Ubuntu: Leaving Trusty
item.description:
As always, posts like these are always to be taken with a few handfuls of salt - they are basically a log of my own trials and tribulations for my own future perusal.
Trusty Tahr, Ubuntu 14.04, ends support next month. Here's what I've learned from the upgrade process, and tips on going to the next release, 16.04 Xenial Xerus.

First of all, for in-place upgrades, you need to go to 16.04 before you go to 18.04. That's the official in-place upgrade process, and it takes a little longer, but you're better off not trying to attach a disk and upgrade from there - it should be possible but you should go the supported way. 16.04 will be around for another two years, so if that's all you need, it's not a bad system to be on.

-Server or Desktop?
Let's agree a desktop is a non-critical user's terminal into network services. If your desktop computer (ATX motherboard in a normal consumer box) is running all the time and running high-uptime services, consider either a) transitioning that service to a server machine, or b) transitioning the box to a server role and using a different system as your desktop.
Desktop systems are either a) laptops that can be offline for long periods of time or b) desk- or floor-based boxes that are not perfomring any critical services provided to other users. These systems should be upgraded fairly frequently to keep up with the latest security patches and technologies. User-operated machines have the largest attack surface (the user, and the keyboard/monitor/mouse) and are important to protect.
All systems I run and administer at this point are on a later desktop OS. This was not an issue at this point.
Servers - find out when an acceptable time exists to turn the server off and break the service for users. It would be ideal to either have a redundant system. If that is not possible, inform the users of a time you're going to take action, and do it. You can't put this off and they have to understand these things need to happen.

-Pre-upgrade Steps
Backup backup backup! Make sure you have copies of all the relevant data from the system easily and readily restorable. For virtual systems, copying the virtual hard drive while the virtual machine is off to a different location on the same host is a good option. Snapshots, provided your systems can work together well, are also a great idea. Win2008R2 does not snapshot Linux machines very well. The snapshots only re-merge while the machine is off, so take that into consideration. VMWare is more Linux-aware, and later versions of Hyper-V (2012R2 and 2016) are very Linux-friendly.
If you cannot back up the system easily, and you don't necessarily know 100% of what is on it, copy /home, /etc, /opt, /var, and any directories that are common for your use case (i.e. if your organization places lots of custom scripts in /usr/local/bin, grab those). Tar them and copy to another machine. Your machine that you're performing the upgrade on is a good bet, if your connection is solid. Without grabbing the entire system, that should conain most of what you need.
Take an output from the package manager (apt list --installed) and look at what package sources are enabled (save the files /etc/apt/sources.list and the directory /etc/apt/sources.list.d/). Go through the output and resolve any inconsistencies before you move on - i.e. if there are old, custom, local-installed packages, make sure you note that and attempt to find the .deb files.
If you have redundancy, point any DNS aliases (box1.service.example.com) at your redundant system to it can take the load and your service doesn't break. That's always a bonus.
Check network interfaces (ifconfig) and MAC addresses if you are rebuilding. Make sure to record that information before you move on.
Check what's running in cron before you leave this old box too.

-Rebuild or Upgrade?
First, determine if your server performs a complex enough role that it needs to be rebuilt. Ubuntu's release upgrader is good enough to where it will ask you what to do with your old configs, but often for newer package versions,
If you are upgrading in-place: update to the latest version of the OS. apt update, apt upgrade, apt dist-upgrade, and apt autoremove. Reboot.
The upgrader will bomb out if you don't have enough space in /boot. You can always find what older kernels are on your system (dpkg -l |grep ^linux-image) and remove what's taking up space. Usually, it's good practice to keep the last two or three known-good old kernels around post-upgrade. That being said, if you're running just fine for weeks on 4.4.0-134, you can throw caution to the wind and remove all but that.

-The Upgrade
Ubuntu documentation starts here. Make sure to do your own research too.
Upgrading is complicated but is mostly handled for you. After you've run through apt dist-upgrade and rebooted, run do-release-upgrade. If you have access to run the upgrade through the hypervisor console, that's helpful - reconnections are easier. If many people access the console on a regular basis, don't do this - you could get interrupted. If you're running over SSH, use Screen/Tmux/Byobu if you want. Easier to reconnect that way.
It will ask you to restart services while running - that's fine; you've blocked out this time to break services anyway. It will show you what packages are being removed/kept/upgraded. This is helpful! Capture this output and save it for future reference, in case something goes wrong.
The upgrade from here on out is simple. The questions you get are along the lines of "should I take the config that is from the new package, or keep your old config?". If you have customized configs for something (say vsftpd or apt or smtpd) you should have done research about new configs and compatibility with your customized config of an old version. Usually these are fine to keep the old ones (with your changes for your environment), but take care to watch after and make sure things don't break.
After upgrading, several packages are listed for removal. That's fine - do that, and then let it reboot the system.
Check to make sure everything's up and running. Tail the logs for your services, and check dmesg. All good? Yay! Nope? Fix it. If you can't fix it, revert. That's why we back up.

-The Rebuild
This is much simpler. Roll out a new machine over top of the old one. Set up its networking, install packages, copy over home directories and custom script files, write in your old configs (don't just drop them in there - you are usually better off adding the custom lines to the new configs in the new format).
Right now, you have an opportunity to rebuild the system how you see fit. The less changes you make from stock, the easier you make it on yourself or anyone else in the future, so remember that and embrace minimal tweaking. On desktop systems, tweak away, but on servers, remember that you have to share the machine and that it must be easy to maintain by others.

-My Experiences
SMTPD
This one was very standard. I upgrade 4 mail relays. They didn't require any manual after-the-fact tweaking post-upgrade and took 16.04 like a champ.
apt-mirror
I have an apt-mirror server to serve packages to other servers behind a firewall in an isolated environment. Getting this server upgraded was easy, and again, took no messing about. I had to add more disk space to the /var disk and extend it, becuase I was going to begin mirroring 16.04 as well. This blog post helped me SO MUCH in getting this server ready to serve upgrades for other machines, though. It's impossible to run a do-release-upgrade normally if you can't hit archive.ubuntu.com, but if you mirror the right parts, in the right places, you can definitely do it right.
Cacti
HAHAHAHAAHA NO
But seriously - cacti 0.8.8f, in 16.04, works fine with PHP5, but not with PHP7, which is the only PHP in 16.04. Also, RRDTool, the backbone for cacti, is upgraded to 1.5 in 16.04, but cacti 0.8.8f only supports RRDTool 1.4.
This was a serious mess. We ended up installing an unsupported version of Cacti from some other Ubuntu version (0.8.8g, the last of the 0.8.8 versions) from the archive.ubuntu.org pool. It required a dep that we pulled from Bionic, but after apt local-installing those packages, Cacti now works fine. There were a few config changes, but that's mostly due to PHP7 changes that cacti's configs made. During the upgrade, these are pointed out, and when you tell it to take the new configs, it saves the old ones right next to them. You'll need to update the password in the config files for cacti post-upgrade, but it should all go smoothly. It was such a ridiculous mess of poorly configured packages and terrible interdependencies that we are going to move to 18.04 as soon as possible, even though that was not in the plan.
bind9
These worked fine, like smtpd. No changes necessary. The version of bind from Trusty to Xenial didn't change that much. If your setup is like mine - master/slave server - upgrade the slave first.

Anyway, that's it for now. It's a really good process, but there are always custom setups to be considered, and that's mainly what I ran into.

(archive.org link of the blog post in case it goes down)

item.title: Simulation Theory
item.description:
The Simulation Hypothesis idea is pretty interesting to me given that I am somewhat knowledgeable about computers, especially Virtualization. Many people think about simulated reality as a Matrix-like system - rooms full of people in coffins or tubes, fed intravenously and brains wired into a collective conscious experience. Like an MMORPG but in reality.

This brings with it the most problems, most notably that people take a lot of energy to maintain, so it is pretty unbelieveable. Second, it begs the question "why". Obviously energy production is a terrible excuse since we'd net negative. Clustered computing is a pretty decent answer, and it does raise quite a few interesting discussion points.

What I'm thinking of, though, is a little more mundane, but pretty sad. This weekend I installed Ubuntu in a virtual machine to test a few things, and of course post-install, as the Desktop variant does, it asked to submit statistics about its installation. It made me chuckle since the machine was wiped only three hours later (testing complete), but it still thought that it was a full-on desktop system. Virtual machines don't usually know they're virtualized - they think they're running on bare hardware, not as a software component on a larger machine. This poor Ubuntu box thought it was going to live a long life on a user's laptop or desktop, and that it would serve them well over the next few years. But it got nuked in less than a day, since I just needed it for a little bit.

So what kind of simulation are we in? Are we a critical infrastructure system, a Windows 2000 install powering something on the power grid? Are we a Red Hat Enterprise Linux 4 box, chugging along unnoticed for years in a corner? Is our simulation more like an IBM Mainframe, running for so long because it has to, or is it more like my Ubuntu VM - just something a kid spins up on his laptop for a few hours to tinker with that gets deleted a few hours later?

Who's running the simulation? Do we assume they care?

item.title: Doing Useful Things
item.description:
Don't waste your time on frivolity. I will detail an experience I had recently where I was confronted with my own such pursuits, and I'll relay to you here what I thought about it.

Over the past two weeks, I've had the pleasure of using a bunch of different systems, configuring them, and messing around with various distros. I decided to take some time to install Gentoo again on my tester laptop, since it's always a fun activity for me. I brought the machine to work after a successful install, intending to tweak a few options during lunch. I sat it on my desk to recompile the kernel and update @world, and I returned to my work.

During the time it was compiling, a coworker walked by my desk. I wasn't interacting with the machine, but the fan was screaming as it used its 4 wimpy threads to crank through compiling Firefox or something. He asked what the heck was going on with this machine, as he knew it was my personal laptop. I told him it was compiling some software, since it was currently running Gentoo. He didn't know Gentoo specifically, so I gave him a 10-second elevator pitch on source-based distros.

Now, this man is a smart man. He's had several decades of experience as our company's network administrator, he's a highly skilled sysadmin, and is always quick on the uptake and sharp as a tack. He always brings an interesting perspective and viewpoint to any discussion, and I have a lot of respect for him. The fact that he didn't really know what Gentoo is specifically essentially says nothing negative about him. If you don't know what Gentoo is either, that doesn't matter.

He gave me a "what the heck are you doing with your life" look, and then asked me if I read every line of source code in Firefox before I set it to compile. "Well, no, but-" "I knew the answer before I asked the question."

What I was doing was useless. It was a waste of time. Gentoo? Really? Chances are, any Linux system that does anything actually important out in the world will be one of these, in order:
Red Hat, SuSE, Ubuntu, CentOS, Debian, and maybe even Fedora and then finally possibly OpenSuSE. (Yes, Alpine and containers, I get it)
The distros that one will never encounter in the wild are, in order of increasing silliness:
Arch, Manjaro, Void, Hannah Montana Linux, and Gentoo.
Gentoo is a beautiful, well put-together, sensible, and clean distro that no one should ever use. OpenRC is the coolest project I've seen in a long time, building from source is really neat, and setting CFLAGS and USE flags are a super customizable way of building your OS exactly how you want it. Gentoo is amazing, but fundamentally, Gentoo is rice and it really isn't useful for 99.999% of people, even people that are interested in using Linux.

If you want to do something useful, install Ubuntu or Debian or CentOS or Fedora and build a web server. Build an email server in a VM. Build a samba server and have your friends pen-test it to see if you can secure it properly. Do something useful with your time. The reason I run mostly Debian/Fedora/CentOS these days is because 1) my systems are old enough where I don't need bleeding-edge kernels for the new hardware drivers, and 2) the firehose of updates that rolling releases provide is really not for most users.

If you want a rolling release, go with OpenSuSE tumbleweed. It's a little different, but dig into YaST and learn something useful. If you like stability Debian and CentOS have you covered! Fedora is pretty close to bleeding edge, but it's the basis for Red Hat, so you'll actually learn something useful about how the most popular enterprise Linux distro works.

Don't spend your time scrolling through Facebook or attempting to use distros that don't make sense or playing bad games or watching bad movies or reading bad books. Especially if you think that it's applicable in a way, step back and think of what someone more experienced would think of what you're doing.

A good indicator: if you're trying to watch an instructional video on Linux Academy or Pluralsight, and you're struggling to get your laptop working with your bluetooth headphones, maybe you've got the wrong distro. Make it get out of your way so you can actually use it. Stop worrying and just use gnome3 and systemd - they're not my favorite, but they're the defaults everywhere and they're well supported and constantly get better. Ubuntu's not my favorite, and I've written about that before, but guess what's on my wife's computer? That's right, 18.04 LTS (standard gnome desktop).

Stop wasting your time distro-hopping, using things that don't really make a difference. Use what works and get your shit done.

item.title: Is this app bad?
item.description:
A family member recently asked me about an app they were using and whether it was trustworthy. Software is pretty scary these days, so here's how you deal with programs you don't trust, and how you learn how to trust people developing programs.

Here is how you operate if you want to run what you believe is "sketchy" software.
1) is it FSF-defined Free Software? If so it is probably safe. https://www.fsf.org/about/what-is-free-software If you can look at the code and contribute to it and talk to the developer, you're probably good.
2) If not Free Software, is it at least Open Source ( https://www.gnu.org/philosophy/open-source-misses-the-point.html ), and is the source code at least available, and is it non-obfuscated? Can you build it yourself? Are the builds "reproducible", and do the binaries you obtain match the binaries you build?
3) Can you run it in a sandboxed/containerized environment? If not, can you spare a separate machine to run it on? Running untrustworthy software containerized (Flatpak, snap, LXD, Docker, etc.) is better than not. Ideally, take a separate machine with no personal data on it, and run the software there. When I have a need to run any Windows program, which is usually non-free software, I run it on a separate physical machine that is not connected to any of my other systems or my network.

Source Code : Compiled programs :: Ingredients : Meals

When you eat, you should know what the ingredients are, and you should trust that the ingredient list at least matches the final meal, even if you can't tell from inspection.

If you have to ask if an app is safe, and if your investigation takes some digging, it's probably not safe. Most platforms offer robust permission controls, but you have to be careful about what permissions an app asks for. A flashlight app does not need access to your contacts for instance. Also, above all, keep your operating system up to date! Programs can exploit flaws in older versions of the OS, since the flaws are made public when updates are issued.

It is very important you read those articles and understand them. You should then search for the source code of programs you are running. If the program you are running is not Free Software it is not trustworthy! You should tell those companies that you don't want their business because they don't respect you as a person. If you don't care enough to share your ingredient list, I won't eat your food!

If one wants to use a computer (that means a cell phone, television, car, a smart speaker, Internet-connected thermostats and doorbells, etc.), one must absolutely understand the security and privacy implications of what they are doing. If not, ignorance makes it worse not only for the ignorer but also for the rest of the world.

item.title: Installing Mac Apps
item.description:
Installing Mac Apps is Mystery and Punishment

People often recommend Apple computers because they're 'easier to use'. I would argue that this is not the case.

A common use case when one obtains a new computer and is setting it up for the first time involves downloading and installing one's favorite programs and configuring the settings the way one prefers. (Side note: Using Time Machine backups does not count; this is behavior exhibited primarily by beginner or first time Mac users. Even many long time Mac owners don't use Time Machine. I've found, as startling as it may seem, backups are primarily used by the tech-savvy, and relatives of the tevh-savvy that have backups foisted upon them.)

Where macOS (formerly Mac OS X) excels in the aspect is settings configuration. For every application there is a global menu, and the Cmd+, shortcut works to open preferences in every default (and most third-party) applications. In each application there is a menu bar item called "Help", in which one can search the entire rest of the menus for commands and command shortcuts. This is, without a doubt, the best feature for newcomers to macOS - the ability to find any action, in any application, anywhere.

System preferences are in one location, which is nice - Windows 10's current mess of half-control-panel-half-modern-settings-app is a far cry from what Control Panel was in Windows 7. Many GNU/Linux desktop environments get it right as well - Gnome3, Cinnamon, and KDE Plasma all have great settings applications that are searchable and efficiently categorized.

------

My main complaint with macOS is application installation. There are too many different ways to perform this simple, regular, and necessary action.

In Windows, an installer is either a .exe or .msi, and both generally walk the user through a wizard with options. The installer can be deleted when finished, and the installed program shows up in a list of all the installed programs in a system settings window, where one would expect.

Many options available on GNU/Linux are even better. Package managers offer a command-line solution to application, library, and component acquisition - often, it's as simple as 'dnf in mpv', or 'pacman -S rhythmbox'. Dependency resolution is automatic (if you've ever seen a dialog pop up during a windows installation complaining about the lack of a .NET framework, this is a huge benefit!) and upgrades of all your system's programs at once is seamless.

In Apple's operating system, we'll break down application acquisition into five sections - Mac App Store, .zip file, .dmg file, .pkg file, and 'other'. 1. The Mac App Store (MAS) is the easiest way to obtain applications. Most users would likely prefer to go this route. However, many developers complain about the lack of control they have in the App Store, how sandboxing is an arbitrary requirement that doesn't really even work, how some updates may just get rejected for unclear reasons, and how long things take to be processed. Many applications (for now; this is changing very soon) like Microsoft Office and the Adobe suite aren't distributed through the MAS either. The revenue model is very greedy, the yearly fees are high for smaller developers, and many quality apps that do alter your system in helpful-but-non-sanctioned ways aren't available on the MAS. Development tools are often left out because of the control they require but can't get. 2. Many applications are distributed in .zip files. This is incredibly efficient, but messy - macOS applications can be entirely a single file, and this is quite nice. The file is a container file for other files, but is viewed by the shell as a launchable program. Many users will unzip an application download in their ~/Downloads folder, leave both files there, and run the application from there. This makes no difference to the system - the appropriate ~/Library folders are still put in place and all configs are stored there - but it creates a messy system filled with unnecessary data, especially with multiple users in mind. (Windows applications can come distributed in .zip files too, but this is much less common, and usually reserved for an alternatively distributed "portable" version that stores its configs in the same folder.) 3. Disk Images (.dmg files) are more or less the exact same. They do, however, come with the built-in ability to customize the background and include a shortcut to /Applications, which many do, in order to hint the user into doing the right thing. This is the case with many advanced or savvy users, but many inexperienced users simply launch the program from the disk image. Again, this is inefficient - effectively they're running a compressed application, and it will perform poorly. Many applications complain about being launched from a disk image. Some even prevent their being launched and instead serve a message about copying a file to their computer, which further confuses users - from their perspective, it's already on their computer! I've seen many Macs with a desktop full of mounted drives running various versions of Chrome and other programs. 4. Packaged application installers are the best way to go. They are a traditional wizard used to place the program into the correct location, run scripts to create folders or place other utilities, or download helper programs to assist with first runs. These are much like Windows installers and generally easy enough to use, but will require administrator access to place files in /Applications. 5. I promised an "other" section so here it is. I did not forget about homebrew and other command-line package managers. They're quite useful and have important features like key checking and unified updating, like many GNU/Linux package managers do. These are not officially supported and often cause more trouble than they're worth.

macOS has come a long way since Mac OS X 10.0. This problem has remained endemic for as long as I can remember, and shows no signs of slowing down - if anything, it will likely fragment further. GNU/Linux has gained Snap, Flatpak, and Appimage in the last decade as well, and these are all in addition to the native package management system. Many of these options seem to try and solve a problem that most people just don't seem to have.

There's no feasible way for Apple to fix this themselves, other than moving applications to /Applications immediately when they're run - there are security implications to this though, and non-admin users wouldn't have the permissions. They've put themselves into a tangle and can't really remove any of the options. In a time gone by when installers seemed like bulk and cruft, and many users just clicked through them without reading anyway, the "download zip, extract, and place app in /Applications" method seemed revolutionary. But people are lazy and skip steps. Where there's an idiot-proof system, there will always be a dumber idiot that breaks it.

item.title: Google Captcha is Malware
item.description:
Google reCAPTCHA v3 is terrible. Initially it looks really cute, with a banner in the corner, and all that, and it's super easy! Just check the box and it works! Unless 1) you don't use Chrome. It heavily favors Chrome users and makes anyone else take many more steps. https://www.cnet.com/forums/discussions/anyone-else-having-issues-with-googles-recaptcha/ 2) they decide to blackhole you (https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/accessible/IhGSH6lMgjI and https://productforums.google.com/forum/#!topic/websearch/duSidFj5r2o) 3) you use any number of popular extensions or browser features to block iframes - basically, a way of sticking another webpage inside your webpage. (You would want to do this for security/privacy reasons; it's a very valid thing to block)

If you end up using umatrix in your browser to block cross-site requests, which I strongly recommend, the web suddenly becomes a more hostile place. It's harder to use any sort of login service. Many login services require an insane amount of cross-site requests, and usually websites host many assets on multiple different domains. Whitelisting becomes a familiar chore, but you end up with a faster browser and less foreign code running on your system.

The biggest pain is reCAPTCHA. This dumb little checkmark that so many websites use causes you so much hassle. I'm at a point where many captchas remain unsolvable for me, because Google thinks I'm a spambot because I don't auto-whitelist all their domains! I'm almost at a point of blocking Google at a router-level and just being done with their bullshit.

item.title: Facebook bans Nazis
item.description:
Today, Facebook, an advertising/malware distribution/surveillance company from California announced it would no longer allow white nationalists (essentially Neo-Nazis as we know them in the States) on their social media platform.

Link here to their post.

Clearly this specific, isolated move is good. We can all agree on that. Because of their absolute control over their communication platform, they have the power to do this.

I hate to be the bearer of bad news - the doom prophet - but sooner or later, something slightly controversial that *you* believe in will also be banned from a platform you use. Be it firearm rights, abortion, religion, conspiracy theories, or encryption/data privacy rights, the ability to express one's voice publicly without being beholden to a Silicon Valley Overloard, is going away. The machine learning algorithms are going to mistake a joke in private message (or group message) between friends as inciting violence and your account will get deleted. Even worse, the police could show up at your door and arrest you for it.

Bruce Schneier wrote about digital feudalism back in 2013. It only seems more relevant now! People are trusing cloud-connected computers to protect their privacy and security, but it all comes down to trusting the companies who have absolute control over them. In the six intervening years since this article's publishing, there's been no reigning in of the power of these monsters. Apple, Google, Amazon, Facebook, and Microsoft have only grown more monstrous and less responsible - less trustworthy.

Taking power away from these companies and putting it in the hands of the users is the most important thing we, as digital citizens, can do. Federation and decentralization is how the Internet was born, and it's the natural way of things. Think of how many social networks and chat/IM systems have come and gone - email, venerable, simple, stoic email, has outlived them all, and shows no signs of stopping. Its nature as a decentralized system makes it more useful and powerful than any other, and its interoperability makes it a first step for everyone on the web.

We need more systems that respect the users and their freedoms, and less that try to take them away. When will people learn that it's dangerous to continue trusting these companies? The only reason they can make moves like this is because of their absolute control, and so many people don't understand that there are other, better choices out there.

item.title: ditch your cell phone
item.description:
Most people who have a traditional computer these days have a laptop. Companies issue their employees laptops, and most students these days buy laptops. People who live in smaller spaces, or can't dedicate a whole desk to a tower and screen, get themselves a portable computer. Desktops aren't very popular these days, and usually those who have a desktop, which tend to be less flexible and more expensive, will have a laptop as well.
