Even laptops are giving way to more portable devices. Tablets and smartphones are becoming increasingly productive, doing more and more every iteration. And everyone these days always carries a smartphone. The connected world, right there in your hip pocket. They can do amazing things: work email on the go, conference calls, mobile hotspot capability for your laptop, check in on your servers from afar, group chat with your international buddies, see your grandma's face from 1,000 miles away, get new music and podcasts on your way to class, and keep up with your family's photo stream all hours of the day. These seem like the perfect devices. Why wouldn't we want one device that can do everything for us?

You've been misled!

Your smartphone is your adversary. They're designed to pull you in, with bright colors, friendly sounds, notifications, and feeds. Their only purpose is to generate more data and revenue from your time, attention, and battery life. This little object doesn't serve you. These apps and feeds are avenues for advertisement, tracking your every move on the web and (sometimes not so) subtlety changing the way your brain works. You need to regain control.

We have so much functionality on these little computers, but if you don't curate your content, it will rule you. If it's not critical to how you work or communicate, get rid of it. Keep your notes app, maybe your music and podcasts, but get rid of news, social media, and games. Liberally apply do not disturb modes, silence your notifications, turn your ringer down low or off. Your phone should be a lifeline, not a television set or news device. The browser is all you should need in a pinch.

Break this little glass square's hold on your brain.

Another way to help you disconnect and retain your sanity with this attention hook in your pocket all day is a color filter.

Turn your phone to grayscale, and keep it that way for a week. You'll find the lack of bright colors make you a little less attracted after a while.

If you want to read, get a book or an e-reader. They won't distract you with incessant notifications about your sister's tweets or your second cousin's 34 photos of his meal. Write a lot? Grab a pen and journal. Want to watch some Netflix? Your laptop or desktop are much larger screens with leagues better speakers. For music, you can't beat a classic iPod. They sound great, and the ping of a text arriving won't interrupt you 15 minutes into Dark Side of The Moon.

My point is, dedicated devices may seem outmoded, bulky, or disconnected, but that's almost the point. Things that matter should have weight in your life. You should feel that journal stretching your back pocket. Your books should take up space in your bag. Your headphones and music player should be a world unto their own, and your camera should make you work for the shot.

The convergence of all this functionality into one tiny orb of distraction has helped us in many ways, and I'm not advocating getting rid of them. They're amazing devices that aid many of us in so many uncountable ways. Just loosen their grip. Be comfortable without them. Recall a time without them, and remember that going outside, being away from home, away from your telephone and internet connection, or even just in the other room, was also being free.

Further reading: How To Break Your Smartphone Addiction Center For Humane Technology Break Up With Your Smartphone

item.title: trust addendum
item.description:
Addendum to 'trust'

There is a developer of a moderately-used set of scripts that I keep up with. He has a website that lists one of those ncurses package installation scripts that's something like "curl https://my.site/install.sh|bash" or whatever. Basically, grab the content of this URL and immediately throw it into a root terminal. (These are a BAD IDEA! But also, if you give someone a harder-than-one-step process to follow, they probably won't follow it. "Paste this into a terminal" is probably too difficult for most users, even.) The software he distributes/develops is good. It's useful and pleasant. It automates installation and configuration of a lot of things for a lot of people. But it's dangerous.

Paraphrasing something this dude has said on an off-the-cuff livestream: "It would be seriously easy for me to put a keylogger [or other malware] into [my software]."

The point he was trying to make - when you install Windows, and you can't see what's in it, how do you know what it does? Microsoft will tell you that the telemtry data includes anonymized statistics, location information, advertising IDs, and other stuff to make your experince better. You can inspect your traffic at your router, or with Wireshark, or whatever. But you can't tear apart those encrypted packets. You can't see what they're sending. Maybe it's keylogger data. Maybe it's your browsing history, piece by piece. Maybe it's hyper-compressed images and audio of your webcam. Who knows? Most people won't check!

Every single line of code in that script you pipe straight into your terminal could be installing malware, replacing your package manager with a version that points to a malicious software source, scraping your SSH keys from your home folder, whatever. It could be doing all of it. It would be so easy to put out a popular piece of software that installed something ever-so-slightly off (say, installing a package from archIinux.org instead of archlinux.org) that you won't notice, and then they own your entire infrastructure!

The point is - check what you run. Read the scripts you execute. Install software that's safe, verified from the appropriate sources. Keep your installation profile to an absolute minimum - do you really need that super-bloated program that pulls in 17 dependencies that you don't know about?

In a computationally centric society, when you don't have control over your computing, whoever does have control over you. Good examples are allowing for-profite businesses to decide what software you can use and therefore how you communicate with others. The same goes for letting others see your private communications or potentially alter the messages in any way. When Company X controls your email, how are you able to use email without them watching? How can your friends and family communicate with you if they don't trust Company X? Free software becomes paramount to freedom at all, in general. It is not foolproof and Free Software alone will not save you, but removing proprietary software from your life is the most basic, necessary starting point, if you wish to be free.

item.title: trust
item.description:
Again, this comes up so often.
Here's a new article about privacy violations by proprietary software. SHOCKER.
Even for companies who are supposedly privacy-forward, their defaults say otherwise. A brand-new iPhone has all these privacy settings that are off by default, and that are usually confusingly labeled and buried several settings screens deep. Once companies have your trust, they can't help but break it if it'll earn them another few bucks. Yelp's a household name and doesn't seem like a bad actor, but that's proven false by this article. Furthermore, while they claim to have your best interests in mind, Apple (and Google) let companies perform this kind of shady behavior on their platforms that they completely control. If they let others get away with this, can you really trust that the "don't upload my photos to your servers" switch really does what it says it does? How do you know your phone isn't recording audio and taking photos to send off to a datacenter in the middle of the night?
This sort of paranoid rabbit-hole diving has kind of taken over my life, but I think it's produced good results. At the end of the day you have to be pragmatic about some things - I the firmware my SSD is running isn't visible to me, but I can have a reasonable assumption that it's not doing anything bad, and the rest of the computer is otherwise pretty verifiable as secure. My phone doesn't get more data than it absolutely needs, and I'm pretty judicious about not using it for stuff. Nothing goes up to cloud services anymore, and everything stays in house. The less reliance that most people have on companies that are just waiting to take advantage of them, the better. I've never been burned myself but I've seen how much damage they can do, and I want nothing of it.

item.title: messaging privacy addendum
item.description:
Update to this thing because I have hooked another Windows/iPhone user on the Free Software drug/religion/cult.
Chrome has a built-in malware scanner that scans your hard drive for malware that targets Chrome, and not much else. Chrome is proprietary software whose code you can't see. Chrome makes a ton of encrypted network requests that you can't see. Chrome is verifiably scanning and reading files on your hard drive and making lots of network requests.
Windows 10 has a keylogger [link 1] [link 2] that is turned on by default. Windows tracks and reports your activity. Windows makes a ton of network requests you can't see. Windows includes a keylogger.
Are you getting it?
You can turn these things off, apparently. There's a button, a switch, a checkbox, whatever. But the software doesn't stop phoning home. The behavior maybe stops. You have no idea. Here's a not-plausible-but-you-can't-prove-me-wrong scenairo - you click the "turn off keylogging" switch, and your computer immediately pings a Microsoft response team, who TURNS UP the keylogging, or highlights your profile in red, or pays closer attention to you, since you care more about privacy. You turn off the iCloud backups on your iPhone, but instead of that, they just exfiltrate tiny packets that they say are "diagnostic and usage data" or whatever, and those are compressed, encrypted packets of keystrokes and sound files from the microphone.
If the operating systems, programs, hardware, and service you use are not open-source, freedom-respecting, privcy-respecting tools, then THEY DO NOT CARE ABOUT YOU. They will do whatever they can to make a buck or to stay in business, whether that means selling your info to advertisers, cooperating with government agencies, or pulling all your data on to their servers just because.
And the best part is - I can provide a hundred examples of how proprietary software abuses you. You can provide NONE where open-source software abuses the rights of the user.
If you're going to live in a techno-centric society (you need smartphones to pay for parking - face it, we're cyborgs at this point) you need to understand how to live in here without getting your rights abused every day by these mega-corps that just want to squeeze every bit of data and money out of you that they can.

item.title: messaging privacy
item.description:
How do you know what you're sending is secure? I got asked recently about iMessage, and if it's 'secure'. I can't give a real answer to that. Apple says it's secure and most people tend to believe them. Here is a 2013 article from TechCrunch on it.
Apple wants you to trust them. They explain their approach to privacy and give you tools to manage it, but again, that requires you to trust what they say. Those switches could do anything. If you trust them, and belive that their servers aren't decrypting your messages and storing them in plaintext, then good for you, you can treat iMessage as a secure messaging platform... if you turn off location services, and if you turn off iMessage backup, and if you turn off iCloud, and if you don't worry about someone tracing your phone number to you, and... the list goes on.
They say that they don't man-in-the-middle all your messages. They say that they don't store information that you don't want them to. "You have control over how your photos...and other data are shared between your devices and iCloud. Individual iCloud services can be turned on or off."
But beliveing all this requires TRUST.
How do you trust opaque services? How do you make sure they're acting in your best interest?
You can't.
In order to trust a service, you have to be able to see their back-end policies, configurations, settings, access controls, and most importantly, the code that runs it. There is absolutely no way to trust the privacy of a system that isn't open-source. It can be independently audited for security, and for a system like Apple's, I imagine it's quite secure - it's a very big target.
No matter what, in the end, you can't consider their systems private because they don't show you how they work. It's a closed system, and for all the toggles and preferences they let you have, you really have no control over anything you give to them, and no way to find out if they're abusing it.
What can you do? Is there a private system out there? Sure. You can use PGP encrpted email (or for that matter, PGP-encrypt ANY text you send over any method), you can use Signal, or you can write letters with personalized ciphers. You can choose to not communicate over networks you don't trust (i.e. any network outside your home). Another good option is using XMPP/Jabber - it's a federated system, like email, so you can use any provider you like, and communicate with anyone else on any other provider, and you can roll your own. There are also many encryption methods that can be applied to XMPP, and it's not tied to a phone number at all.
You must be able to personally audit systems that you trust with private information. If your providers don't allow you to look at what they're running, it's probably because they don't want you to find out.

item.title: x220 vs x230
item.description:
For the past 6 months I have been using a Thinkpad x230 (running the Skulls distribution of Coreboot for the BIOS) and it's been great. As a direct upgrade from my x220, it's fantastic - uses mostly the same internal components, has a better cooler, much better CPU/GPU, USB3 (my x220 is not the i7 variant with USB3), and even fits in the same dock. The day I got it, I flashed Skulls to it, then popped my SSD and Atheros wi-fi card in, and it worked the exact same as the x220.
One thing continues to turn me off about the x230, and that's the keyboard. It's not like it's bad - it's still a best-in-class keyboard, has a backlight and the thinklight, and the trackpoint is also pretty good. But coming from an x220 and a Cherry MX desktop keyboard, it's a long step down. I missed the x220 keyboard too much. It's more solid and less wobbly, the mouse buttons are better, and the whole layout is much more desktop-like. It's THE top-tier laptop keyboard! Nothing even comes close to this model. (OK, my Dell Latitude E6500 comes close, but that thing's a beast and way too big to be used as a portable.)
So now I'm back on the x220 and the x230 is sitting on the sidelines. There are various "x220 keyboard on x230" guides out there but the extra performance isn't critical for me, since I'm not running Gentoo or playing games.
Anyway, the x220 is a great machine. Without a doubt the best computer I've ever owned, even if far from the most powerful. Coreboot just makes it that much better. The x230 is also a great machine, but the beautiful keyboard (and the blue enter key!) keep me coming back to the x220 despite the clear drawbacks in other areas.
My recommendations:
If you're familiar with classic Thinkpads and like this layout, get the x220. Best and last of the good classic Thinkpads.
If you're coming from a Dell or Macbook or something, literally anything will be an upgrade, so get the x230. The clear benefits of a better GPU and USB3 are hard to ignore. Might as well not ruin yourself on an ancient keyboard you'll never be able to walk away from.

item.title: arch-linux-usage
item.description:
Whoops. I did a thing. I decided to give Arch a go.


I've never seriously used Arch Linux on my own hardware. I've played around with a VM, run Antergos before (hilariously it was my primary machine while I was job searching, even though it was pretty ridiculous), but never seriously used Arch long-term. I installed it on my test laptop a week ago, and with no outstanding issues, dropped it on my primary machine. Woah.


It's pretty neat. There's not much to say - it's not an opinionated distro. That's my main problem with Ubuntu and others - they've got their logo plastered all over the OS and it's got their colorscheme everywhere. Debian and Fedora tend to be my favorites, but Fedora's "I'm not a rolling release seriously trust me plz" model is really weird to me. Plus dnf is sloooooooow.


Arch is mostly what I want. I'll probably dabble with Parabola because ~FREEDOM~, but I'm really enjoying a rolling release with both current packages and no opinionated branding. Arch is basically 100% upstream with no tweaks and no changes. It doesn't feel like a cohesive unit, but more like a minimal system with the option of adding a bunch of extra packages on top. The installation just requires you to partition things the way you want, and pretty much does the rest for you, with a ton of really useful tools along the way. Pacstrap is like a good version of a Gentoo stage3, wifi-menu is like an easy version of wpa_supplicant, and pacman is...
well....


Pacman is the one area where arch falls down. It's fast and configurable and really easy to work with, but man is it a little sticky. When using dnf to remove a package, it removes its unneeded dependency packages at the same time. Debian's apt will mark them as such, and you can 'autormeove' them later. Here's three commands that all do the same thing:


1) apt autoremove
2) dnf autoremove
3) pacman -R $(pacman -Qtdq)


Seriously, I don't think it'd be that hard to write a package manager with syntax that uses actual words instead of switches. That is my one major complaint about Arch. But hey, at least it's fast, mirrors are easy to switch between, and its keyring system is very simple to understand!

------


Anyway, Arch is a great distro. No SELinux or AppArmor by default, but that's fine. Nothing but the barebones, raw, lean, simple setup that you need, and then you can add what you want after. I'm a tentative convert. I know it's not a useful system to be using, but I've cut my teeth on Debian- and Fedora-based systems for a while now, and I think it's fine to use this as long as it doesn't get in my way. The minute I have to struggle with something simple like printing or nework config, it's going in the trash and I'm going back to Fedora or Debian. But using a full DE like Gnome3 (it's really really fast now!) mitigates any issue I have. I encourage any readers to try it, becuase it lets you do what you want, instead of dumping a full set of gnome-games on you (debian) or preinstalling an office suite and mail/chat/calendar programs you won't use.


I'll add a script later to get things set up, but getting a new Arch system running to satisfaction is really easy. Just a few pacman, useradd, and systmectl commands, and you've got a full usable system, with wifi, printing, bluetooth, power management, and all that.

item.title: My writing in 2020
item.description: I've decided to re-instantiate a good chunk of my old posts (written from 2012-2019 under a pseudonym) here. They may not be properly dated. Furthermore I'm going to continue writing here under my real name, because I miss doing that. Stay tuned.
